heroOverview: Hemorrhoids are enlarged or displaced vascular cushions in and around the anal canal that affect an estimated 75% of people at some point in their lives, making them one of the most common anorectal conditions worldwide. The diagnosis of unspecified hemorrhoids (ICD-10: K64.9) is applied when the degree or exact location has not been fully characterised. These dilated venous plexuses produce symptoms ranging from painless rectal bleeding on toilet tissue to persistent anal itching, mucous discharge, and, in more advanced cases, prolapsed tissue that must be reduced manually. While hemorrhoids rarely threaten life, they cause substantial everyday discomfort and lead roughly 1 million Americans to seek medical care each year. Effective treatments now exist for every stage, from simple dietary measures that relieve mild symptoms in weeks to office-based procedures and surgery that resolve advanced or refractory disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Symptomatic hemorrhoids affect an estimated 10-15% of adults at any given time; around 75% experience hemorrhoids at least once (American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence between ages 45 and 65; more frequent in pregnancy and immediately postpartum
keyStats.avgAge: Presentation peaks in the fourth to sixth decades of life
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 1 million outpatient visits per year in the United States alone

definition: Hemorrhoids (ICD-10: K64.9, unspecified) are pathologically enlarged or prolapsing vascular and connective-tissue cushions of the anal canal. Under normal anatomy, three main cushions at the 3, 7, and 11 o'clock positions contribute to anal continence by providing a compressible seal. Pathological hemorrhoids develop when the supporting submucosal connective tissue degenerates — partly from chronic straining, increased intra-abdominal pressure, or obstructed venous return — allowing the cushions to engorge, slide distally, or prolapse through the anal opening. Internal hemorrhoids arise above the dentate line and are covered by rectal mucosa, meaning they contain no pain fibers and therefore bleed without pain. External hemorrhoids develop below the dentate line in anoderm that is richly innervated, causing pain — especially when thrombosed. The unspecified designation applies when a clinician documents hemorrhoidal disease without specifying the degree (first through fourth) or laterality. Gastroenterologists and colorectal surgeons manage the condition; for most patients in primary care the diagnosis is clinical.

typesClassification.description: Arise above the dentate line, bleed painlessly, and are graded by their tendency to prolapse: grade I stays within the anal canal; grade II prolapses on straining but reduces spontaneously; grade III requires manual reduction; grade IV is permanently prolapsed and cannot be reduced.
Develop below the dentate line in pain-sensitive anoderm. Usually asymptomatic unless they thrombose, in which case a tender, firm, purple swelling appears at the anal verge.
Involve both above and below the dentate line; combine the bleeding of internal disease with the pain potential of external disease.
An acutely clotted external hemorrhoid producing sudden, severe anal pain and a palpable swelling; can be treated by incision and evacuation within 72 hours for fastest relief.
Coded when documented hemorrhoidal disease has not been further classified as to degree, location, or type — common in initial clinical encounters and screening visits.

primarySymptoms: Bright-red, painless rectal bleeding noticed on toilet tissue or dripping into the toilet bowl, typically after a bowel movement — the hallmark of internal hemorrhoids.
Anal itching or pruritus ani, often caused by mucous seepage or moist skin around an internal hemorrhoid that has partially descended.
A sensation of incomplete bowel emptying or a feeling of something bulging at the anal opening during or after defecation.
Visible or palpable prolapsed tissue at the anus, ranging from a small soft lump after straining to a permanently protruding mass.
Mucous or slimy discharge from the anus that may stain underwear and cause perianal skin irritation.
Dull perianal discomfort or heaviness, particularly after prolonged sitting or after a large bowel movement.
Acute, severe anal pain with a firm tender swelling at the anal verge, indicating a thrombosed external hemorrhoid.
Faecal soiling or difficulty keeping the anal area clean, especially when prolapsed internal hemorrhoids impair sphincter function.
Mild anaemia in patients with chronic, repeated rectal bleeding — presenting as fatigue, pallor, and breathlessness rather than obvious haemorrhage.
Perianal swelling that reduces spontaneously after straining or that the patient pushes back manually.
earlyWarningSigns: A smear of bright red blood on toilet tissue after a single straining episode.
Mild perianal itching or dampness at the end of the day.
A soft, reducible bulge felt at the anal verge only during defecation.
Occasional mucous staining of underwear without pain.
emergencySigns: Heavy rectal bleeding that does not stop or is producing large blood clots — warrants same-day emergency assessment.
A permanently prolapsed hemorrhoid that cannot be reduced manually and is becoming ischaemic or necrotic (dark purple to black tissue).
Acute severe anal pain with a tense, blue, golf-ball-sized swelling that developed within 24-48 hours, consistent with a large thrombosed external hemorrhoid.
Rectal bleeding in any patient over 40 who has not had a recent colonoscopy, given the need to exclude colorectal cancer.
Signs of anaemia — dizziness, breathlessness, or syncope — alongside ongoing rectal blood loss.

causes.description: Repeated Valsalva manoeuvres during defecation raise intra-abdominal pressure, impede venous drainage of the anal cushions, and gradually stretch and weaken the connective tissue anchoring them in place, the central mechanism in most symptomatic hemorrhoids.

Passage of infrequent, hard stools both prolongs toilet time and directly traumatises the anal mucosa, increasing bleeding risk and mechanical stress on the cushion suspensory ligaments over months to years.

causes.cause: Chronic diarrhoea or frequent loose stools
causes.description: High stool frequency increases the number of defecatory events per day, cumulative straining episodes, and mucosal irritation, explaining why haemorrhoids are common in both constipation and diarrhoea-predominant gut disorders.

The growing uterus compresses the inferior vena cava and pelvic veins, raising venous pressure in the haemorrhoidal plexus; progesterone-driven smooth muscle relaxation further impairs venous tone, making symptomatic haemorrhoids nearly universal in the third trimester.

causes.cause: Low-fibre diet and inadequate fluid intake
causes.description: Insufficient dietary fibre reduces stool bulk and softness, leading to harder stools and longer colon transit times; each of these increases straining frequency and is a well-established, modifiable cause of haemorrhoidal symptoms.

Sitting on a toilet for extended periods — particularly while reading or using a smartphone — eliminates the support the toilet rim would provide and allows the anal cushions to engorge under the influence of gravity and vascular pressure.

With age, the fibromuscular supporting apparatus of the anal cushions loses elasticity, allowing normal vascular tissue to slide downward even without exceptional straining; this partly explains the rising prevalence between ages 45 and 65.

riskFactors.description: Diets providing less than 15 g of fibre daily are consistently associated with constipation, straining, and haemorrhoidal symptoms; increasing intake to 25-35 g/day reduces recurrence and postoperative bleeding.
Habitual straining at stool, regardless of its cause, is the single most frequently cited modifiable risk; bowel habit normalisation is the cornerstone of medical management.
Pregnancy raises pelvic venous pressure substantially; most pregnancy-related haemorrhoids improve within weeks of delivery, but they may persist or recur in subsequent pregnancies.
Anal cushion connective tissue degenerates with age; prevalence rises steeply from the forties onwards, with a peak between 45 and 65 years.
Increased intra-abdominal pressure from excess adipose tissue and higher rates of constipation in obesity both raise haemorrhoidal risk; weight loss is a recognised preventive measure.
Desk-bound occupations and extended toilet sitting increase pelvic venous pressure and reduce the muscular activity that assists venous return; regular movement reduces risk.
Frequent loose stools and accompanying urgency increase mechanical trauma and straining cumulative load on the anal cushions.
Familial clustering suggests an inherited component to connective-tissue quality, though the relative contribution of shared diet and bowel habits is difficult to separate.

affectedDemographics: Pregnant women in the second and third trimesters
Postpartum women, in whom symptoms often peak in the first weeks after delivery
People with sedentary occupations who sit for long hours
Individuals with chronic constipation, IBS, or inflammatory bowel disease
Patients who are obese or significantly overweight
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of haemorrhoids is primarily clinical and begins with a focused history: the duration of rectal bleeding, its relationship to defecation, presence of prolapse, itching, pain, and any change in bowel habit. On examination, the perianal skin is inspected for skin tags, excoriation, or visible external haemorrhoids; asking the patient to strain often reveals prolapsing internal tissue. Digital rectal examination assesses sphincter tone and detects masses but cannot reliably detect soft internal haemorrhoids. Proctoscopy or anoscopy — a brief, usually painless outpatient procedure — is the definitive way to visualise internal haemorrhoids and grade them by extent of prolapse. Any patient over 40 presenting with rectal bleeding for the first time, or at any age with a change in bowel habit, unexplained anaemia, or family history of colorectal cancer, requires colonoscopy or flexible sigmoidoscopy to exclude a proximal neoplasm before attributing bleeding solely to haemorrhoids. Full colonoscopy is recommended in individuals aged 45 and over as part of standard colorectal cancer screening. The key differential diagnoses include anal fissure (which causes pain with defecation distinct from the painless bleeding of internal haemorrhoids), perianal abscess, colorectal cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and rectal prolapse.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the anal canal and lower rectum to confirm internal haemorrhoids, grade their severity, and rule out other anorectal pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short, lubricated rigid tube about 10 cm long is gently inserted into the anus with you lying on your side; it is mildly uncomfortable but usually painless and takes 2-5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses sphincter tone, detects palpable masses or rectal polyps, and provides initial information about the anal canal before instrumentation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gloved, lubricated finger is inserted briefly into the rectum; mild pressure is felt but it should not cause sharp pain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Examines the sigmoid colon and rectum to exclude colorectal polyps or cancer when bleeding is the presenting complaint.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera is passed into the lower bowel after a small enema; mild cramping is typical, and the procedure takes 10-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Full examination of the colon to exclude neoplasm, inflammatory bowel disease, or other proximal bleeding sources in patients over 40 or with risk factors.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Requires a bowel preparation the day before; performed under sedation, taking 20-40 minutes; bloating and cramping resolve quickly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects iron-deficiency anaemia from chronic rectal blood loss — a finding that also prompts investigation for a proximal bleeding source.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results available within hours and may reveal low haemoglobin or mean cell volume.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies blood in stool that is not macroscopically visible; used in colorectal cancer screening programmes alongside symptom evaluation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect a small stool sample at home and return it to the laboratory; no preparation or clinic visit is needed.

treatmentOverview: Management is stepwise, beginning with conservative measures and escalating to procedures or surgery only when symptoms persist. For most patients with mild to moderate haemorrhoidal symptoms, increasing dietary fibre to 25-35 g per day and fluid intake to 1.5-2 litres daily reliably softens stools and reduces straining; randomised trials show this halves recurrent bleeding in internal haemorrhoids. A regular bowel routine — avoiding prolonged toilet sitting and responding promptly to defecatory urge — reinforces fibre therapy. Topical preparations containing corticosteroids, local anaesthetics, or astringents provide short-term symptomatic relief of itch and perianal irritation but do not treat the underlying vascular cushion. When conservative measures fail, office-based procedures are the next step. Rubber band ligation is the most effective non-surgical treatment for grades I-III internal haemorrhoids, achieving long-term relief in about 80% of patients; it is performed in the outpatient clinic without anaesthesia. Sclerotherapy (injection of a sclerosant solution) is a rapid, safe alternative particularly suited to grade I-II internal haemorrhoids with significant bleeding. Infrared coagulation is used for similar indications. Surgical haemorrhoidectomy is reserved for grade III-IV internal haemorrhoids that have failed office treatment and for thrombosed or mixed haemorrhoids with significant external components; it achieves the lowest recurrence rate but carries the highest risk of short-term pain. Stapled haemorrhoidopexy and haemorrhoidal artery ligation with mucopexy are less painful alternatives with comparable efficacy for selected internal haemorrhoids. Acutely thrombosed external haemorrhoids presenting within 72 hours can be relieved by incision and evacuation under local anaesthesia.

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary fibre supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Soluble and insoluble fibre supplements (psyllium husk, methylcellulose) or increased dietary fibre from fruit, vegetables, and wholegrains soften stools, reduce straining time, and lower recurrence of bleeding. Guidelines recommend 25-35 g daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show approximately 50% reduction in bleeding and prolapse symptoms compared with placebo; effective in over 70% of grade I-II patients when combined with adequate hydration.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical haemorrhoidal preparations
medicalTreatments.description: Over-the-counter creams and suppositories containing lidocaine, hydrocortisone, or witch hazel (hamamelis) relieve perianal itching, burning, and mild swelling. They do not shrink internal haemorrhoids and should not be used for more than 7-10 days continuously.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief of itching and discomfort in roughly 60-70% of patients in short-term use; no effect on recurrence or underlying pathology.

medicalTreatments.name: Sitz baths and warm soaks
medicalTreatments.description: Sitting in warm (37-40°C) water for 15-20 minutes twice daily reduces internal anal sphincter spasm, cleanses the perianal skin, and provides analgesic relief for thrombosed or prolapsed haemorrhoids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Consistently rated effective for symptom relief in observational studies; reduces pain scores by approximately 30-40% in acute thrombosis when used as adjunct to other treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Osmotic or stool-softening laxatives
medicalTreatments.description: Polyethylene glycol, lactulose, or docusate sodium soften stools when fibre alone is insufficient, reducing the mechanical trauma of each bowel movement. Used short-term during acute episodes or perioperatively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hard stools and straining in nearly all patients with constipation-related haemorrhoids; clinical improvement rates of over 80% when combined with fibre therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Flavonoids (diosmin-hesperidin)
medicalTreatments.description: Micronised purified flavonoid fractions improve venous tone and lymphatic drainage; meta-analyses of randomised trials show reduced bleeding episodes and prolapse symptoms in internal haemorrhoids, particularly useful as adjunctive treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: A Cochrane-reviewed meta-analysis found approximately 58% reduction in bleeding episodes and 65% reduction in prolapse episodes vs. placebo over 3-6 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Rubber band ligation
surgicalOptions.description: A rubber band is placed at the base of an internal haemorrhoid above the dentate line, cutting off its blood supply; the tissue sloughs over 7-10 days. Performed in the outpatient clinic without general anaesthesia; one to three bands per session, sessions separated by 4-6 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves symptom control in approximately 75-85% of grade I-III internal haemorrhoids; recurrence rate around 30-40% at 5 years, lower than other office procedures.

surgicalOptions.name: Sclerotherapy
surgicalOptions.description: Injection of a sclerosant (phenol in oil, or sodium tetradecyl sulfate) into the submucosa above the dentate line induces fibrosis and obliterates the feeding vessel. Painless, rapid, and suitable for grades I-II or patients on anticoagulants.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial haemostatic response in over 90% of grade I-II bleeding haemorrhoids; approximately 70% remain symptom-free at 1 year; recurrence higher than banding for grades II-III.

surgicalOptions.name: Infrared coagulation
surgicalOptions.description: Infrared energy coagulates the haemorrhoidal stalk, causing fibrosis and fixation; effective for grades I-II with bleeding. Procedure takes seconds per application with minimal discomfort.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Short-term bleeding control in approximately 80% of cases; slightly less durable than rubber band ligation for grade II-III haemorrhoids.

surgicalOptions.name: Conventional haemorrhoidectomy (excision)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of haemorrhoidal tissue under regional or general anaesthesia (Milligan-Morgan or Ferguson techniques); the definitive procedure for grade III-IV haemorrhoids with large external components or after failure of office-based treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term resolution in over 90-95% of cases; recurrence rate of about 5% at 5 years; associated with significant postoperative pain lasting 1-3 weeks.

surgicalOptions.name: Stapled haemorrhoidopexy (PPH)
surgicalOptions.description: A circular stapling device repositions and resects the prolapsing mucosa above the dentate line, interrupting haemorrhoidal blood supply without excising external haemorrhoids; less painful recovery than conventional haemorrhoidectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable long-term efficacy to excisional haemorrhoidectomy for internal haemorrhoids; recurrence and reoperation rates slightly higher (around 15% at 5 years in some series).

alternativeTreatments.name: Increased fibre from whole foods
alternativeTreatments.description: Adding oats, legumes, linseed, and skin-on vegetables to the diet achieves the same stool-softening benefit as supplements with the added advantages of improving gut microbiome diversity and reducing colorectal cancer risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback for pelvic floor dysfunction
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients who strain because of dyssynergic defecation rather than constipation, biofeedback retraining of pelvic floor muscles can eliminate the root cause without medication; evidence supports it as a first-line approach in this subgroup.

alternativeTreatments.name: Horse chestnut seed extract (aescin)
alternativeTreatments.description: Has mild venotonic activity and is used in some European countries to reduce venous fragility and oedema; a small number of randomised trials suggest modest reduction in bleeding and swelling, though evidence quality remains limited.

whySeeSpecialist: A colorectal surgeon or gastroenterologist should be consulted when rectal bleeding persists despite six to eight weeks of conservative treatment, when haemorrhoids prolapse permanently or cannot be reduced, when office-based procedures have not provided relief, when thrombosis is severe or recurrent, or when the diagnosis is uncertain. Any patient over 40 presenting with first-time rectal bleeding needs specialist review to exclude a proximal neoplasm by colonoscopy before the bleeding is attributed to haemorrhoids alone.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a colorectal surgeon or gastroenterologist with dedicated anorectal experience who performs rubber band ligation and other office procedures in the clinic rather than only offering hospital-based surgery. Ask whether the practice routinely performs anoscopy and flexible sigmoidoscopy at the initial visit. For patients who are also due colorectal cancer screening, a gastroenterologist who can combine diagnostic colonoscopy with the haemorrhoid consultation provides efficient care.
preventionStrategies: Consume 25-35 g of dietary fibre daily through whole grains, legumes, fruit, and vegetables to maintain soft, easy-to-pass stools.
Drink 1.5-2 litres of fluid daily; adequate hydration is essential for fibre to work effectively.
Respond promptly to the urge to defecate — resisting it leads to harder, drier stools and increased straining.
Limit time spent sitting on the toilet to two to three minutes per visit; avoid using your phone or reading material in the bathroom.
Exercise moderately for at least 30 minutes on most days to stimulate bowel peristalsis and reduce pelvic venous pressure.
Maintain a healthy body weight, since obesity raises intra-abdominal pressure and the risk of constipation.
Treat constipation early with fibre and fluids before it becomes chronic and begins to damage anal cushion connective tissue.
lifestyleModifications: Establish a regular bowel routine — going at the same time each day reduces irregular straining.
Increase walking and aerobic activity to promote peristalsis.
Replace processed grains with whole grains and add a daily portion of beans or lentils.
Avoid sitting on the toilet for more than two to three minutes; a small footstool to elevate the knees can facilitate more natural defecation posture.
Do not strain; if a bowel movement is not forthcoming in two minutes, leave and return later.
Keep the perianal area clean and dry without using harsh soaps or vigorous wiping.
Wear loose cotton underwear and avoid tight synthetic clothing that traps moisture around the anal area.

recommended: Soluble fibre sources: oats, psyllium husk, flaxseed, apples, pears, and legumes to soften stools
Insoluble fibre sources: wholewheat bread, bran, broccoli, and carrots to add bulk and reduce transit time
Water: at least 6-8 glasses daily to keep fibre effective
Probiotic foods such as yoghurt, kefir, and fermented vegetables to support healthy bowel motility
Berries and citrus fruit for their flavonoid content, which may reduce venous fragility
avoid: Low-fibre ultra-processed foods, white bread, and white rice that slow transit and harden stools
Excess alcohol, which dehydrates and can worsen constipation
Spicy foods in large amounts if they trigger diarrhoea or perianal irritation in sensitive individuals
Excessive caffeine, which can dehydrate and irritate the bowel

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise — walking, swimming, or cycling for 30 minutes most days — is beneficial: it stimulates colonic motility, reduces constipation risk, and helps manage body weight. Heavy weightlifting, rowing, or any activity requiring a prolonged Valsalva manoeuvre should be avoided by anyone with symptomatic haemorrhoids, as sustained straining raises intra-abdominal pressure markedly. After haemorrhoid banding or surgery, light walking from day one is encouraged but strenuous exercise should be deferred for two to four weeks.
dailyManagement: Take a fibre supplement or ensure a fibre-rich meal at every breakfast to regulate bowel habit.
Keep a small footstool in the bathroom to elevate the knees and ease defecation.
Apply a plain fragrance-free barrier cream after each bowel movement if perianal skin is sore.
Record any episodes of bleeding, noting whether they are increasing in volume or frequency.
Seek prompt review if a lump appears at the anus and cannot be gently pushed back, as this may indicate irreducible prolapse or thrombosis.
Complete the full course of any prescribed laxative or flavonoid treatment even after bleeding stops.
prognosis: The outlook for unspecified haemorrhoids is generally favourable. The majority of patients with mild to moderate symptoms achieve satisfactory relief within four to eight weeks of dietary modification and avoidance of straining, without needing procedures. Rubber band ligation resolves symptoms in around 80% of patients with grades I-III internal haemorrhoids, though roughly one third will have a recurrence over five years that may require repeat banding. Surgical haemorrhoidectomy for advanced disease carries a long-term cure rate above 90-95%. Pregnancy-related haemorrhoids usually resolve spontaneously within one to three months postpartum. The main risk in unmanaged haemorrhoids is progressive prolapse from grade I to grade IV and chronic iron-deficiency anaemia from persistent low-grade bleeding, both of which require more intensive intervention.
recoveryTimeline: After rubber band ligation, mild discomfort and a feeling of fullness last 24-72 hours and resolve without specific treatment; the sloughed tissue passes unnoticed at 7-10 days. After haemorrhoidectomy, postoperative pain is most intense in the first week and requires regular analgesia; most patients return to light activity in 1-2 weeks and full normal function in 3-4 weeks. Constipation is common in the early post-operative period and must be actively managed with fibre and stool softeners to prevent wound disruption.
complications: Iron-deficiency anaemia from chronic, unrecognised rectal blood loss — requires iron supplementation and treatment of the haemorrhoid.
Irreducible prolapse with strangulation — a grade IV haemorrhoid becomes ischaemic and requires urgent surgical assessment.
Thrombosis — an acutely clotted external haemorrhoid causes severe pain; if untreated it resolves over 2-4 weeks but is very uncomfortable.
Post-procedural pain and anal spasm following rubber band ligation or haemorrhoidectomy — usually manageable with analgesia and warm sitz baths.
Faecal incontinence in rare cases where repeated procedures or surgery damage the internal anal sphincter.
Perianal sepsis — a rare but serious complication of rubber band ligation, presenting within a few days as perineal pain and fever requiring emergency surgical drainage.

supportResources.name: American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons — Hemorrhoids
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient guide on diagnosis, grading, and all available treatments from the leading U.S. colorectal surgery specialty body.

supportResources.name: NHS — Piles (haemorrhoids)
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK patient guide covering symptoms, self-care, and when to see a GP.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Hemorrhoids
supportResources.description: Overview of haemorrhoids from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases, including treatment options.

sources.title: Lohsiriwat V. Hemorrhoids: from basic pathophysiology to clinical management. World J Gastroenterol, 2012
Sneider EB, Maykel JA. Diagnosis and management of symptomatic hemorrhoids. Surg Clin North Am, 2010
American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons — Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Hemorrhoids (2018)
Alonso-Coello P et al. Fiber for the treatment of hemorrhoids complications: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Am J Gastroenterol, 2006
Simillis C et al. A systematic review comparing medical, nonoperative, and operative treatments for hemorrhoids. Dis Colon Rectum, 2015
NHS — Haemorrhoids (piles): diagnosis, treatment, and complications

confusedWithConditions.name: Anal fissure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An anal fissure causes sharp, tearing pain at the moment of defecation that persists for minutes to hours; internal haemorrhoids bleed painlessly. Both may coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rectal prolapse
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rectal prolapse involves full-thickness protrusion of the rectum beyond the anus, producing concentric mucosal rings, whereas prolapsed haemorrhoids form discrete cushions with radial folds.

confusedWithConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Colorectal cancer may also cause rectal bleeding but is typically accompanied by a change in bowel habit, weight loss, or tenesmus; colonoscopy is required to distinguish the two in patients over 40.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anal abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An anal abscess causes constant, throbbing perianal pain with a tender, fluctuant swelling; haemorrhoidal pain is intermittent and related to defecation unless thrombosis has occurred.

relatedConditions.name: Anal fissure
relatedConditions.relevance: Anal fissures frequently co-exist with haemorrhoids and share constipation as a primary cause.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Rectal bleeding attributed to haemorrhoids requires exclusion of colorectal cancer by colonoscopy in adults over 40.

relatedConditions.name: Other hemorrhoids (K64.8)
relatedConditions.relevance: A related classification used for specified hemorrhoidal conditions not separately classified.

relatedConditions.name: Ulcerative colitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Colonic inflammation in ulcerative colitis may coexist with or mimic haemorrhoidal bleeding, requiring colonoscopy for accurate differentiation.

faqs.question: What do hemorrhoids look like?
faqs.answer: Internal hemorrhoids are not visible and can only be seen with an anoscope; they appear as soft purple-red cushions above the dentate line. External hemorrhoids look like small skin-coloured or purple swellings at the anal verge. A thrombosed external hemorrhoid is a firm, tender, blue-purple lump.

faqs.question: Are hemorrhoids dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most hemorrhoids are not dangerous. They cause discomfort and bleeding but rarely threaten life. However, persistent bleeding can cause iron-deficiency anemia, and an irreducible prolapsed hemorrhoid can become strangulated and require urgent surgery. Any rectal bleeding in a person over 40 needs investigation to exclude colorectal cancer.

faqs.question: Can hemorrhoids go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Mild internal hemorrhoids often improve significantly with dietary changes and stool softening within 4-8 weeks. Thrombosed external hemorrhoids typically resolve over 2-4 weeks as the clot is reabsorbed, though they are painful in the interim. Grade III-IV internal hemorrhoids are unlikely to resolve without a procedure.

faqs.question: How do I know if my rectal bleeding is from hemorrhoids or something more serious?
faqs.answer: Hemorrhoidal bleeding is typically bright red, appears on toilet tissue or drips into the bowl at the end of a bowel movement, and is painless. Dark or mixed-in blood, blood with mucus, unexplained weight loss, or a change in bowel habit suggests a more serious cause and needs colonoscopy. See a doctor for any first-time rectal bleeding if you are over 40.

faqs.question: Do hemorrhoids cause cancer?
faqs.answer: No. Hemorrhoids do not cause or become colorectal cancer. However, the two conditions share the symptom of rectal bleeding, so it is important not to assume all bleeding is from hemorrhoids without appropriate investigation, particularly in adults over 40 or those with risk factors for colorectal cancer.

faqs.question: What is the fastest way to relieve hemorrhoid pain?
faqs.answer: A warm sitz bath for 15-20 minutes provides fast relief of anal spasm and pain. A topical lidocaine cream can numb the area temporarily. For an acutely thrombosed external hemorrhoid presenting within 72 hours, incision and evacuation by a clinician gives the most rapid relief of pain.

faqs.question: Is surgery the only way to get rid of hemorrhoids permanently?
faqs.answer: No. Rubber band ligation in the outpatient clinic resolves grades I-III internal hemorrhoids permanently in about 75-85% of patients without surgery. Surgical hemorrhoidectomy is the most durable option for advanced or recurrent disease and achieves long-term cure in over 90% of cases.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with hemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: Yes. Moderate aerobic exercise such as walking, swimming, or cycling is beneficial and helps regulate bowel habit. Avoid heavy weightlifting or any activity requiring sustained straining, as this increases intra-abdominal pressure and aggravates symptoms. After surgical treatment, defer strenuous exercise for 2-4 weeks.

faqs.question: Does eating more fibre really help hemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: Yes. Randomised trials show that increasing dietary fibre to 25-35 g daily reduces hemorrhoidal bleeding by approximately 50% and decreases prolapse symptoms. Fiber works by softening stools and reducing the need to strain. Psyllium husk supplements are an easy way to reach the target if diet alone is insufficient.

faqs.question: What does unspecified hemorrhoids (K64.9) mean?
faqs.answer: The ICD-10 code K64.9 means hemorrhoidal disease has been diagnosed but not classified into a specific degree (grade I through IV) or exact type at the time of coding. It is commonly used at an initial visit before full assessment or when the clinical notes do not specify the grade.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter hemorrhoid creams long-term?
faqs.answer: Over-the-counter creams containing hydrocortisone should not be used for more than 7-10 days continuously, as prolonged steroid application can thin perianal skin. Plain barrier creams and sitz baths can be used indefinitely. If symptoms require cream for longer than two weeks, see a clinician for a procedure-based solution.

faqs.question: Are hemorrhoids more common in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hemorrhoids are nearly universal by the third trimester of pregnancy because the growing uterus compresses pelvic veins and progesterone relaxes smooth muscle, impairing venous tone. Most pregnancy-related hemorrhoids improve significantly within 1-3 months after delivery with conservative measures.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for hemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if rectal bleeding is new and you are over 40, if bleeding is heavy or not stopping, if a lump cannot be pushed back in, if you have significant pain, or if symptoms have not improved after 4-6 weeks of dietary changes. Any change in bowel habit alongside bleeding also requires medical review.

faqs.question: What is rubber band ligation and how effective is it?
faqs.answer: Rubber band ligation places a small elastic band around the base of an internal hemorrhoid, cutting off its blood supply. The tissue dies and falls off within 7-10 days. It is performed in the clinic without general anesthesia and resolves symptoms in approximately 75-85% of grade I-III cases.

faqs.question: Can hemorrhoids cause constipation?
faqs.answer: Hemorrhoids do not directly cause constipation, but pain from a large or thrombosed hemorrhoid can make patients reluctant to defecate, which worsens constipation and creates a cycle. Conversely, chronic constipation is a primary cause of hemorrhoids; breaking the cycle with stool softeners and fibre is essential.

faqs.question: Will hemorrhoids come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Hemorrhoids can recur after any treatment if the underlying causes — low fibre, straining, constipation — are not corrected. Recurrence after banding is about 30-40% at 5 years; after surgical hemorrhoidectomy it is around 5%. Long-term dietary changes and bowel habit modifications substantially reduce the risk of recurrence.

faqs.question: Is witch hazel effective for hemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: Witch hazel (hamamelis water) applied topically acts as an astringent and mild anti-inflammatory, reducing perianal itching and swelling in mild hemorrhoids. It does not treat internal hemorrhoids or prolapse but is a safe, inexpensive short-term comfort measure with minimal side effects.

faqs.question: Can children get hemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: Hemorrhoids are uncommon in children but can occur, usually in association with chronic constipation or straining. In children, rectal bleeding should always prompt a thorough evaluation to exclude other causes such as rectal polyps or inflammatory bowel disease. Management starts with dietary fibre and stool softeners.

faqs.question: What is the difference between internal and external hemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: Internal hemorrhoids arise above the dentate line in pain-insensitive mucosa and typically bleed painlessly. External hemorrhoids develop below the dentate line in pain-sensitive anoderm and cause pain, especially when thrombosed. Mixed hemorrhoids involve both zones and combine both sets of symptoms.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after hemorrhoid banding?
faqs.answer: Most patients experience mild fullness or pressure for 24-72 hours after rubber band ligation and return to normal activities the following day. The sloughed tissue passes unnoticed at about 7-10 days. A small amount of bleeding at 7-10 days is normal. Severe pain or fever after the procedure requires immediate medical attention.

metaDescription: Hemorrhoids cause rectal bleeding, anal itching, and prolapse. Learn grades, causes, treatments from banding to surgery, and prevention tips. Doctor-reviewed.
